questions.question: An email says 'Your account will be SUSPENDED in 2 hours unless you verify now!' What is the safest response?
options: Click the link to prevent suspension
Call official support to verify your account status
Reply asking for more time to respond
Forward to your manager to handle

questions.question: Why do attackers create extreme urgency in phishing messages?
options: To help you respond faster to real threats
To bypass your critical thinking and verification habits
To comply with data protection regulations
To test your email response time

questions.question: Your 'manager' texts: 'EMERGENCY! Wire £5,000 NOW — no time to call!' What do you do?
options: Send the money — it's from your manager
Wire half first as a cautious test
Call your manager on their known verified number
Ask HR to process it immediately

questions.question: A pop-up reads 'VIRUS DETECTED — Call this number IMMEDIATELY!' You should:
options: Call the number to protect your device
Grant them remote access as they suggest
Close it and contact your IT security team directly
Download their recommended cleaning tool

questions.question: Which message is a clear urgency manipulation red flag?
options: A scheduled IT maintenance notice
A meeting invite for next week
A message demanding action in 15 minutes or your account is permanently deleted
A product newsletter with a seasonal offer

questions.question: What is the best first step when any message creates panic or extreme urgency?
options: Act quickly to prevent the stated consequence
Slow down and independently verify the request
Forward it to your whole team immediately
Delete the message to avoid infection

questions.question: An IT email says 'Respond within 10 minutes or your access will be permanently revoked.' This is:
options: Standard IT security procedure
A legitimate automated security alert
Likely a phishing attempt using artificial urgency
A valid warning from your security team

questions.question: A caller says your bank account is being drained and needs your PIN to stop it. You:
options: Hang up and call your bank's official number
Provide just the last 4 digits as a compromise
Ask them to hold while you check your balance online
Transfer funds to a 'safe account' they provide

questions.question: Urgency-based social engineering succeeds because it:
options: Provides verifiable evidence of a real threat
Bypasses rational thought by triggering fear and panic
Always comes with official supporting documentation
Is easy to trace back to the real sender

questions.question: The single most effective defence against urgency manipulation is:
options: Having a fast email response system
Pausing and verifying through an independent channel
Ignoring all messages that have deadlines
Only reading emails during business hours

questions.question: An email from 'CEO@company-secure.co' asks you to bypass approvals for an urgent payment. You:
options: Comply — it came from the CEO
Verify by calling the CEO's office on their known direct number
Reply asking for written approval by return email
Process half the amount as a compromise

questions.question: What is a Business Email Compromise (BEC) attack?
options: When company email servers are hacked by ransomware
A spam campaign flooding a business inbox
Attackers impersonating executives to authorise fraudulent transfers
When employees accidentally email confidential data externally

questions.question: A caller claims to be from Microsoft and needs your password to fix a problem. You:
options: Hang up — Microsoft never calls unsolicited asking for passwords
Provide a temporary password instead
Give only your username for identification
Ask them to send an official email request

questions.question: An 'HR Director' emails: 'Update your bank details for payroll via this link.' This is likely:
options: Standard HR procedure for payroll updates
A payroll diversion fraud attempt
An automated payroll system update
A routine annual data verification exercise

questions.question: Which is the clearest sign of an authority impersonation attack?
options: Email from your confirmed manager about a known project
A letter on official company letterhead
A calendar invite from a known colleague
Email from an unfamiliar domain asking you to act urgently on behalf of an executive

questions.question: 'IT Security' emails requesting you to reset your password urgently via a link. You should:
options: Click the reset link in the email immediately
Navigate directly to the official IT portal to reset your password
Reply with your current password for identity verification
Forward the warning email to all staff

questions.question: Why are authority-based phishing attacks particularly effective?
options: They always contain perfect spelling and grammar
They only target senior management
Most people are conditioned to comply without questioning authority
They are always sent from verified email addresses

questions.question: A 'senior partner' calls asking you to share client data immediately for an audit. You:
options: Verify their identity through official company channels before sharing anything
Share the data — internal audits are legitimate
Email the data to their stated address and CC your manager
Ask a junior colleague to handle the call

questions.question: How do you best verify a suspicious email claiming to be from an executive?
options: Check that it contains the company logo
Look for a professional signature at the bottom
Count the number of recipients in the CC field
Check the actual sender domain and verify via a separate trusted channel

questions.question: An 'executive' email asks you to keep the request secret from the security team. This is:
options: Acceptable for sensitive business matters
Only suspicious if the amount exceeds your spending policy
Standard practice in merger and acquisition processes
A significant red flag — legitimate business requests rarely bypass security review

questions.question: An email says 'YOU HAVE WON a £500 gift card! Click here to claim!' You:
options: Delete it — you did not enter any prize draw
Click to see what you have won
Forward it to a friend who might want it
Reply with your address to receive the gift card

questions.question: Why do attackers use reward-based lures in phishing emails?
options: To provide genuine incentives to cooperation
To test employees' reactions to good news
To exploit natural human optimism and lower the victim's guard
To comply with marketing data collection regulations

questions.question: An internal email announces a cash bonus — click this link to confirm your details. You:
options: Click immediately to secure your bonus
Ignore it — you were not expecting a bonus
Verify by checking the official HR or payroll portal directly
Reply asking for the bonus amount before proceeding

questions.question: Which is the clearest red flag in a reward-based phishing email?
options: A professional-looking company logo
A formally written email
Correct spelling and grammar throughout
An offer requiring you to enter personal details via a link to claim a prize

questions.question: A supplier offers an exclusive discount for 24 hours — click the link to activate. You:
options: Ignore all discount offers as they are always fake
Click immediately so you do not miss out
Verify the offer directly with the supplier by calling their known number
Forward to your entire purchasing department

questions.question: You land on a page saying you are the '1,000th visitor' and have won a prize. This is:
options: A legitimate website milestone celebration
A random lucky event worth exploring
Likely a fake prize page designed to steal your personal data
A standard marketing survey campaign

questions.question: Which behaviour best protects you from reward-based social engineering?
options: Only accepting rewards from organisations you already know
Clicking links to check whether rewards are real
Reporting all prize notifications to your IT team
Being sceptical of any unsolicited offer requiring you to take action or provide data

questions.question: An email says your loyalty points will expire unless you click to redeem them now. You:
options: Click the link to redeem your points before they expire
Contact the company directly via their official website or app
Ask a colleague to check the link safety first
Ignore all loyalty programme communications

questions.question: Reward lures in cyberattacks primarily exploit:
options: Greed in high-earners only
Employees who frequently shop online
Natural human optimism and the desire for gain regardless of context
Financial vulnerability in low-income workers

questions.question: Before clicking any link in a reward or offer email, the most important check is:
options: Whether the email has a professional signature
Whether the email appears to be from a known brand
Whether the reward amount sounds reasonable
Whether the link URL matches the sender's stated domain

questions.question: An email says your data will be published online unless you pay £200 in Bitcoin within 48 hours. You:
options: Pay immediately to prevent public exposure
Contact your IT security team and report the extortion attempt
Pay a reduced amount to negotiate
Ignore it completely — they definitely do not have your data

questions.question: Why are fear-based threats effective in cyberattacks?
options: They always include real evidence of compromise
They are sent from verified government addresses
Fear impairs rational thinking and causes people to act without verifying
Most people have genuinely compromising data to protect

questions.question: Your screen displays: 'ALL FILES ENCRYPTED — PAY NOW!' You:
options: Disconnect from the network immediately and report to IT security
Pay the ransom quickly to restore your files
Restart your computer to remove the encryption
Try to decrypt the files yourself using online tools

questions.question: A caller claims to be from HMRC saying you owe unpaid taxes — pay now or be arrested. You:
options: Pay immediately to avoid arrest
Ask them to email the official notice
Provide your bank account number to confirm your identity
Hang up — HMRC does not make unsolicited calls demanding immediate payment

questions.question: Regarding ransomware payments, which statement is CORRECT?
options: Paying always guarantees full file recovery
Paying does not guarantee recovery and directly funds further criminal attacks
Ransomware only affects government and hospital systems
Restarting your PC removes most ransomware infections

questions.question: An email claims you have violated copyright law and must pay a fine via a link or face prosecution. You:
options: Pay the fine to avoid legal trouble
Click to read the full legal notice carefully
Verify with your legal team or check official government channels
Forward to your manager to handle urgently

questions.question: Fear-based social engineering is most dangerous because it:
options: Is exclusively used in targeted spear phishing attacks
Always delivers technical malware directly
Triggers panic that overrides security training and critical thinking
Is undetectable by all modern security tools

questions.question: A browser pop-up warns your computer is severely infected — call a support number immediately. You:
options: Close the browser and run your official company antivirus software
Call the support number as instructed to get help
Click on the pop-up to read the full infection report
Download the cleaning tool they advertise in the pop-up

questions.question: You receive an email threatening to send embarrassing photos to your contacts unless you pay. You:
options: Report it to the police and your IT security team — do not pay
Pay a small amount to test if they go away
Reply asking for proof that they actually have the material
Warn all your contacts preemptively about the threat

questions.question: The best technical defence against ransomware is:
options: Installing the cheapest antivirus available
Keeping all software unpatched for system stability
Regular tested offline backups combined with up-to-date endpoint protection
Only storing important files on cloud services
